ETC Scans Main
General Note
Forms part of the Rose Goldsen Archive of New Media Art.
General Note
See also: ETC digital collection, which consists of digitized videos from this collection.
Scope and content
Collection contains files related to programs and events sponsored or supported by ETC, grant proposals and reports, research files, publications from the ETC library, files on individual artists and institutions, taped interviews, and recorded conferences and workshops.
Preferred Citation
Experimental Television Center records, #8229. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.
Biographical / Historical
The Experimental Television Center (ETC) was founded in Owego, N.Y. in 1971 as an outgrowth of a media access program established by Ralph Hocking at Binghamton University in 1969, to provide support and services to the media arts community. ETC's mission was to encourage the creation of work using new electronic media technologies; to encourage an informed appreciation of media art; and to help honor our independently created moving-image heritage by initiating projects and participating in partnerships which address the needs for research, education and preservation, and place independent works within a larger cultural context.
Conditions Governing Access
Access to specific folders in Series I, II, and V must be requested a week in advance.
Interactive multimedia. Paik, Nam June Wright, Walter Vasuka, Steina Vasulka, Woody Sykes, Barbara Wooster, Ann-Sargent Hill, Gary Hocking, Sherry Miller Bode, Harald
7523132 <<<<<<< HEAD Series I. Programs and events
Arrangement
Original arrangement. The folders are organized by program, report, activity, and correspondence, mostly chronological.
Processing Information
Boxes 60 and 61: Folder titles created by staff (Alana Staiti)
Scope and Contents
This series contains promotional materials (flyers, posters, programs, press releases, articles) and planning materials (correspondence, reports, meeting minutes) related to the events that the Experimental Television Center (ETC) and other affiliated institutions (e.g. Visual Studies Workshop) have organized or participated in.
Conditions Governing Access
Access to folders in this series must be requested a week in advance.ETC Programs and Events1971-1972601-2

Scope and Contents
This series contains applications, budgets, reports, and correspondence related to granting agencies including New York State Council for the Arts (NYSCA) and the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA). These grants are used primarily for artist residency, program, event, and research. The series also contains materials related to ETC's own residency program, presentation fund, finishing fund, electronic arts grant, etc.
Conditions Governing Access

Scope and Contents
Interview. The research project concerned the design and creation of electronic moving-image instruments by independent artists and engineers, and the place of this work within the context of video art history. The project included original research, including audio interviews and the creation of original informational and other texts, and the compilation of existing descriptive materials. The notes on the audiotapes were written by Jon Burris, a researcher with the project. Scope and Contents
This series includes documents (resumes, biographies, proposals, manuscripts, artist statements, etc.), artworks, (photographs, negatives, drawings, prints, etc.), and promotional materials (programs, posters, flyers, etc.). Scope and Contents
Copy handbook.Series VII. Discussions and interviews
Scope and Contents
This series contains audio and video tapes of interviews and conversations with various artists. Transcripts and notes are sometimes provided. It also contains ETC's telephone logs (31 spiral notebooks with telephone messages from various artists).
The material in Box 106-A, Experimental Television Center with the Vasulkas, Interviews by Jon Burris was created with a grant from the National Endowment for the Arts. The research project concerns the design and creation of electronic moving-image instruments by independent artists and engineers, and the place of this work within the context of video art history. The researchers include Woody Vasulka, Steina Vasulka, Ralph Hockings, Sherry Miller Hocking, and Jon Burris.ETC Telephone Logs1973-199062

In this subseries, materials are related to the conference Video History: Making Connections at Syracuse University. Lectures and discussions aimed to explore how this new medium of art, activism, and information was developed by artist innovators, video collectives, new technology manufacturers, funders, museum curators, and public television producers.Video Rewind: A Seminar on Early Video HistoryV-10642; V-10643; V-106441998-10-16-1998-10-18108VHSBoyle, DeirdreLondon, BarbaraRyan, PaulTeasdale, ParryJohn HanhardtV-10645Undated108Media Infrastructure Conference SessionV-106461998-10-16-1998-10-18108Hanhardt, JohnHill, ChrisTeasdale, ParryEbrahimi, Sara ZiaFalling Forward Gracefully: Cultural Context and Audience Conference SessionV-106471998-10-16-1998-10-18108VHSFung, RichardHigh, KathyRa'ad, WalidTool Developments/People and MachinesV-106481998-10-16-1998-10-18108VHSBowman, GregGeiger, CarlHocking, RalphJones, DavidMcCoy, JenniferMcCoy, KevinVasulka, SteinaResource/Tool RoomV-106491998-10-16-1998-10-18108

Scope and Contents
This series contains promotional materials (flyers, posters, programs, press releases, articles) and planning materials (correspondence, reports, meeting minutes) related to the events that the Experimental Television Center (ETC) and other affiliated institutions (e.g. Visual Studies Workshop) have organized or participated in.
Conditions Governing Access
Access to folders in this series must be requested a week in advance.ETC Programs and Events1971-1972601-2

Scope and Contents
This series contains applications, budgets, reports, and correspondence related to granting agencies including New York State Council for the Arts (NYSCA) and the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA). These grants are used primarily for artist residency, program, event, and research. The series also contains materials related to ETC's own residency program, presentation fund, finishing fund, electronic arts grant, etc.
Conditions Governing Access
Access to folders in this series must be requested a week in advance.Proposal for Expansion of the Experimental Television Center, NYSCA1970-1971617

Scope and Contents
This series includes bibliographies, syllabi, notes, manuscripts, interviews, and other sources related to a variety of research projects by Woody Vasulka, Steina Vasulka, Ralph Hocking, Sherry Miller Hocking, among others.
The subseries National Endowment for the Arts Funded Research Project in Box 117, 27-66; Box 1118, 1-8, consists of research projects supported by the National Endowment for the Arts. Scope and Contents
Interview. The research project concerned the design and creation of electronic moving-image instruments by independent artists and engineers, and the place of this work within the context of video art history. The project included original research, including audio interviews and the creation of original informational and other texts, and the compilation of existing descriptive materials. The notes on the audiotapes were written by Jon Burris, a researcher with the project. Scope and Contents
This series includes documents (resumes, biographies, proposals, manuscripts, artist statements, etc.), artworks, (photographs, negatives, drawings, prints, etc.), and promotional materials (programs, posters, flyers, etc.). Media materials such as digital prints, texts, videos, scanned ephemera are also included.Tape Catalog Project NYSCAExpression901

Scope and Contents
Copy handbook.Series VII. Discussions and interviews
Scope and Contents
This series contains audio and video tapes of interviews and conversations with various artists. Transcripts and notes are sometimes provided. It also contains ETC's telephone logs (31 spiral notebooks with telephone messages from various artists).
The material in Box 106-A, Experimental Television Center with the Vasulkas, Interviews by Jon Burris was created with a grant from the National Endowment for the Arts. The research project concerns the design and creation of electronic moving-image instruments by independent artists and engineers, and the place of this work within the context of video art history. The researchers include Woody Vasulka, Steina Vasulka, Ralph Hockings, Sherry Miller Hocking, and Jon Burris.ETC Telephone Logs1973-199062
